Streaming Tokens Over a Named Pipe — A Field Note

Last month I rebuilt the transport layer of a local assistant so that generated tokens stream over a Windows named pipe instead of a localhost socket. The change sounds cosmetic, but it removed an entire class of firewall prompts and made the client connect reliably on machines with aggressive endpoint protection. This note records the two traps that cost me an evening each.

The first trap is framing. A named pipe gives you a byte stream, and if you write JSON messages back to back, the reader will eventually receive one and a half of them. The fix is the oldest one in the book: a four-byte big-endian length prefix on every frame, validated against a hard cap before you allocate anything.

def read_frame(stream) -> bytes:
    header = stream.read(4)
    length = int.from_bytes(header, "big")
    if length > MAX_FRAME_BYTES:
        raise FrameTooLarge(length)
    return stream.read(length)

The second trap is flushing. The writing side buffered eagerly, and short frames sat in the buffer until the next token arrived, which made streaming look jittery even though generation was smooth. Flushing after every frame fixed the perceived latency completely; the throughput cost was unmeasurable at chat scale.

If you take one thing from this note, take the cap-then-allocate ordering. Validating the length prefix before allocation is the difference between a protocol error and a denial of service, and it costs a single comparison. The full harness, including the failure-injection tests, is linked from the archive page.
